one_line_takeaway: Simple, imperceptible character-level edits (especially homoglyph substitution) exploit tokenization fragility to remove LLM watermarks far more efficiently than token- or sentence-level attacks, and a genetic-algorithm attack guided by a cheaply trained reference detector extends this advantage to realistic, query-limited black-box settings -- even against adaptive defenses.
key_finding: Character-level perturbations consistently and substantially outperform token-level and sentence-level attacks at matched/low editing rates: e.g., at ER=0.1 on KGW/SynthID/Unigram, character-level attacks achieve average ASR 0.1672 versus 0.0740 for token-level (roughly 2.3x). Detector AUC after character-level attacks falls to 0.9833/0.8244/0.6861 at ER=0.1/0.3/0.5 respectively, versus 0.9887/0.8556/0.7294 for token-level attacks at the same rates (original detector AUC ~1.0 before attack). Homoglyph substitution is the single most effective character-level operation because it most reliably splits a token into 3+ sub-tokens. Human evaluation gave watermarked text an overall quality score of 2.626, sentence-level attacks 2.476, character-level attacks 2.235, and token-level attacks only 1.263 (0-3 scale), i.e., character-level attacks preserve text quality far better than token-level ones. Under the more constrained AC2 setting with a 1500-query budget, the GA-guided character-level attack achieves ASR 0.6514 (OPT) / 0.6615 (LLaMA), clearly beating Best-of-N (0.4414 / 0.4466 at N=10) and the adapted Sand baseline (0.2622 / 0.3099); gradient-based transfer attacks (TextBugger, DeepWordBug) via the reference detector achieve ASR < 0.1 across all schemes, confirming the paper's claim that gradient-based guidance is unreliable due to reference/original detector mismatch. Reference detector quality (more augmentation, Ref-0 to Ref-9) monotonically improves downstream ASR for GA and Best-of-N. Against the four proposed defenses, the adaptive compound character-level GA attack retains high ASR (e.g., 0.9125 vs 0.4214 for plain GA on KGW under the SC-augmented detector; still 0.4776-0.95 range across schemes against OCR-augmented detection), supporting the paper's 'adversarial dilemma' claim that no fixed defense reliably stops adaptive character-level attacks.
core_contribution: The paper (NDSS 2026) presents a systematic study of LLM watermark removal attacks under two realistic, constrained black-box threat models. Its own contributions, distinct from the surveyed related work it critiques (prior removal attacks assuming unlimited detector/LLM queries or surrogate LLM access), are: (1) a formalization of two threat models -- AC1 (no access to the original watermark detector, only limited queries to the victim LLM) and AC2 (limited black-box queries to both victim LLM and its detector); (2) an 'attack range' analysis showing that character-level perturbations (typos, deletions, swaps, zero-width/whitespace insertions, homoglyph substitution) disrupt tokenization and thus affect more tokens per edit than token-level or sentence-level perturbations, and a random character-level baseline attack built on this insight; (3) under AC2, a Genetic-Algorithm-guided removal attack that trains a lightweight BERT-based 'reference detector' (regression model approximating the true detector's global watermark score) from a limited query budget, with three stabilization mechanisms -- filtering high-gradient tokens, a two-stage optimization objective, and a convergence threshold -- to counteract the mismatch between reference and original detectors; (4) an empirical 'adversarial dilemma' argument and an adaptive compound character-level attack (combining multiple character perturbations at the same position, e.g., swap+homoglyph) that is formalized as a two-level optimization and evaluated against four candidate defenses (spell-checking, OCR, Unicode normalization, anomalous-character deletion).
threat_model: Black-box adversary with access only to the victim LLM's output text (no model weights, logits, architecture, or watermark-scheme knowledge), restricted to a limited number of queries per input. Two capability levels are defined: AC1 (System Model 1) -- no access at all to the original watermark detector, attacker can only apply perturbations blindly; AC2 (System Model 2) -- the watermark detector is a public API and the attacker gets a limited per-input query budget to it. The adversary's goal is formalized as minimizing the editing rate ER(X, X~) subject to the perturbed text's global watermark score Sw(X~) falling below the detection threshold tau_d.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) the reference detector is inherently mismatched with the true rule-based/scheme-specific original detector, causing it to be 'overly sensitive to high-gradient tokens' -- this is why plain gradient-based optimization on the reference detector fails (ASR<0.1) and why the authors had to add filtering/two-stage/convergence-threshold heuristics rather than relying on gradients directly. (2) DIPPER's editing rate can be roughly tuned (~0.5) but AuthorMist's cannot, making sentence-level comparisons at matched ER only approximate. (3) Character-level attacks fragment tokens, which lowers ROUGE-F1 (n-gram overlap metric) even when semantic fidelity (BLEU, human ratings) is comparable, an artifact the authors explicitly flag rather than a hidden weakness. (4) For 'weaker' watermark schemes (DIP, Unbias), token-level and character-level attacks perform similarly, meaning the character-level advantage is scheme-dependent, most pronounced for KGW/SynthID/Unigram at low ER. (5) The offline reference-detector training itself costs 5000-50000 queries depending on augmentation level, a cost the paper argues is amortizable but which is nonetheless a real resource requirement not present in the completely feedback-free AC1 setting.

datasets_used: RealNewsLike subset of C4 (prompts for watermarked text generation)
5000 watermarked + 5000 non-watermarked samples per scheme (reference detector training set)
quantitative_results: Character-level vs token-level ASR at ER=0.1 on KGW/SynthID/Unigram: 0.1672 vs 0.0740 (average)
Detector AUC after attack at ER=0.1/0.3/0.5: character-level 0.9833/0.8244/0.6861 vs token-level 0.9887/0.8556/0.7294 (original AUC ~1.0)
Human evaluation overall quality (0-3 scale): watermarked 2.626, sentence-level 2.476, character-level 2.235, token-level 1.263
AC2 GA-based character attack ASR: 0.6514 (OPT) / 0.6615 (LLaMA), vs Best-of-N(N=10) 0.4414/0.4466, vs Sand 0.2622/0.3099
Gradient-based transfer attacks (TextBugger, DeepWordBug) on reference detector: ASR < 0.1 for all five watermark schemes
Reference detector query cost: Ref-0/5/9 require 1/6/10 queries per sample -> 5000/30000/50000 total queries for 5000 samples
Runtime: Best-of-N < 1s/sample; GA < 30s/sample (<20 iterations, 1-3s each) on an NVIDIA A100
Adaptive compound GA vs plain GA ASR against SC-augmented detector on KGW: 0.9125 vs 0.4214
Average token length in C4: 5.18 characters per token
baselines_compared: Best-of-N attack (N=10 and N=1500) using the reference detector
Gradient-based adversarial text attacks: TextBugger, DeepWordBug
Frequency-based zero-feedback attack (token-frequency heuristic, no detector access)
